content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation and GitHub repositories,
in the Agent SDK Overview covers
the complete process of installing the Claude Agent SDK for Python and TypeScript, along with configuring your development environment and API credentials
: "The Claude Code CLI is automatically bundled with the package - no separate installation required! The SDK will use the bundled CLI".
: "Make sure your local project environment is set up and dependencies are installed in the right folder".
: "So the package appears in your local node_modules and can be imported correctly".
: "Set your API key as an environment variable".
: "The SDK also supports Claude Code's filesystem-based configuration".
# No separate check needed - bundled with package

from claude_agent_sdk import Agent

# Create agent
agent = Agent()

# List files (test built-in tools)
agent.list_files(".")
Package not in local node_modules: ensure project directory setup
: "We previously tried installing the SDK globally but hit module errors" — install locally, not globally.
Modify source files, create new code
: "Your agent can start working immediately without you implementing tool execution".
Think of installation and setup like

: you download the OS (install SDK), connect to Wi-Fi (set API key), and then you're ready to use all the apps that come pre-installed (built-in tools). You don't need to build the camera app (tool execution) — it's already there. Just turn it on and start using it.
Prerequisites: Python 3.10+ or Node.js 18+, Anthropic API key from platform.anthropic.com
Local installation: install in project directory, not globally (avoid module errors)
Built-in tools: file reading, command execution, code editing, web search — no implementation needed
Verify: Import package and test with built-in tools (no errors)
Benefits: agent starts working immediately, no tool execution implementation needed
"Your agent can start working immediately without you implementing tool execution" — the SDK bundles everything needed, so after installation and API key setup, you're ready to build agents.
Understanding the internals of installation & setup helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with installation & setup has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying installation & setup in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Installation & Setup is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Installation & Setup is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Before installing the Anthropic SDK, verify that your development environment meets the minimum requirements. The Python SDK requires Python 3.8 or later; check with python3 --version. Python 3.11+ is recommended for production because of significant performance improvements to the interpreter and asyncio scheduler. You also need pip 21.0+ for reliable dependency resolution — older pip versions sometimes install incompatible transitive dependencies. An active Anthropic API key is required; obtain one from console.anthropic.com by creating an account, adding a payment method, and generating a key under API Keys. API keys begin with sk-ant- and are 108 characters long. Keys are displayed only once at creation time — if you lose a key, revoke it and create a new one. Never share keys in code repositories, Slack messages, or public pastebin sites.
Python 3.8+ required; 3.11+ recommended for production performance. Verify: python3 --version
pip 21.0+ for reliable dependency resolution. Upgrade: python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
Virtual environment strongly recommended to isolate SDK and its dependencies from system Python.
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable must be set before importing the SDK.
Outbound HTTPS to api.anthropic.com on port 443 must be permitted by your firewall or corporate proxy.
For corporate networks, obtain the proxy URL and CA certificate bundle before attempting installation.

Install the SDK inside a virtual environment to prevent version conflicts with other Python projects on the same machine. Virtual environments create an isolated Python interpreter with its own site-packages directory, so upgrading the SDK in one project does not break another. The standard library venv module is sufficient for most cases. Alternatively, poetry and conda provide dependency locking and environment reproducibility — poetry.lock files guarantee that every developer on a team installs identical transitive dependency versions. For Docker-based deployments, add RUN pip install anthropic to your Dockerfile after copying requirements files, and do not use virtual environments inside Docker containers (the container itself provides isolation). When using pip, always pin the major and minor version in requirements.txt (e.g., anthropic>=0.25,<0.26) to avoid surprise breaking changes from a minor upgrade.
pip in venv: python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install anthropic
poetry: poetry add anthropic — generates a poetry.lock for reproducible installs across machines.
conda: conda create -n myenv python=3.11 && conda activate myenv && pip install anthropic
Docker: RUN pip install anthropic==0.25.* in Dockerfile; no virtual environment needed inside containers.
Version pinning: anthropic>=0.25,<0.26 in requirements.txt prevents automatic major-version upgrades.
Extras: pip install "anthropic[vertex]" installs Google Cloud Vertex AI support; "anthropic[bedrock]" for AWS Bedrock.

API key security is non-negotiable: a leaked key can drain your account budget and expose your usage patterns to adversaries. The golden rule is that your API key should never appear as a literal string anywhere in your codebase. Instead, load it exclusively from the environment using os.environ or a dedicated secrets library. For local development, create a .env file in your project root containing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... and load it at application startup with python-dotenv. Add .env to your .gitignore immediately — committing a key to git history is difficult to fully remediate because the key remains visible in git log even after deletion. For staging and production, use your cloud provider secrets manager: AWS Secrets Manager, Google Secret Manager, or Azure Key Vault all provide versioned secret storage with fine-grained IAM access controls and automatic rotation support.
Never hardcode keys as string literals — use os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"] or python-dotenv for all environments.
Add .env to .gitignore before creating the file; run git log to verify no key has been committed accidentally.
If a key is accidentally committed, revoke it immediately on console.anthropic.com and generate a replacement — git history purging is not sufficient.
Production secrets: use AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or HashiCorp Vault with IAM-restricted access.
Rotate keys every 90 days as a hygiene practice; update CI/CD pipelines and production secrets in tandem.
Avoid ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in CI/CD environment variables that are logged — use masked variables in GitHub Actions secrets instead.

Enterprise environments often route outbound HTTPS traffic through a corporate proxy that performs TLS inspection, replacing the server certificate with an internal CA certificate. The Anthropic SDK uses httpx for HTTP transport, which respects the standard HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, and NO_PROXY environment variables. If TLS inspection is active, you must configure the custom CA bundle so that httpx trusts the inspected certificates: set the SSL_CERT_FILE environment variable to point to your corporate CA bundle PEM file. For environments where the proxy adds latency, increase the SDK timeout from the default 600 seconds using the timeout parameter on the client constructor. For custom API base URLs — for example when using Anthropic API via a corporate gateway or Vertex AI — pass base_url to the client constructor.
Proxy via environment: export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.example.com:8080 before running Python.
Custom CA bundle: export SSL_CERT_FILE=/etc/ssl/certs/corp-ca-bundle.pem for TLS-inspecting proxies.
Timeout configuration: client = Anthropic(timeout=120.0) sets 120-second timeout; use httpx.Timeout for per-call granularity.
No-proxy list: export NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1 to bypass proxy for local services.
Debug HTTP traffic: set HTTPX_LOG_LEVEL=debug to log all requests and responses for troubleshooting.

After installation and configuration, run a verification script that exercises the API end-to-end before building application logic. A good verification script checks that the SDK imports correctly, the API key is loaded from the environment, a simple API call succeeds with the expected response structure, streaming works, and the model version you intend to use is accessible. Common error conditions at this stage include AuthenticationError (wrong or missing key), RateLimitError (account lacks quota), ConnectionError (proxy or firewall blocking outbound HTTPS), and NotFoundError (model ID typo). Verify each condition by checking the error message — the SDK provides descriptive exception classes that identify the root cause without requiring you to inspect raw HTTP responses.
ImportError on import anthropic: SDK not installed in current virtual environment; run pip install anthropic.
AuthenticationError: check that ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set and starts with sk-ant- — copy-paste errors are common.
RateLimitError: your account may lack credits; add a payment method on console.anthropic.com.
ConnectionError or SSLError: corporate proxy blocking outbound HTTPS or TLS inspection without CA bundle.
NotFoundError on model ID: verify the model string matches the current model catalog (models change periodically).
After successful verification, print the usage field from the response to confirm token counting works for cost tracking.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Setup verification script for Anthropic SDK.
Run: python verify_setup.py
Expected output: Anthropic SDK setup verified successfully.
"""
import os
import sys

# Step 1: Check environment variable
api_key = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
if not api_key:
    print("ERROR: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not set.")
    print("Set it with: export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...")
    sys.exit(1)

if not api_key.startswith("sk-ant-"):
    print(f"WARNING: API key does not start with 'sk-ant-'; got prefix: {api_key[:10]}...")

print(f"API key found: {api_key[:12]}...{api_key[-4:]}")

# Step 2: Import SDK
try:
    import anthropic
    print(f"SDK version: {anthropic.__version__}")
except ImportError:
    print("ERROR: anthropic package not installed.")
    print("Install with: pip install anthropic")
    sys.exit(1)

# Step 3: Create client
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Step 4: Make a simple API call
try:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=64,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Reply with just the word: verified"}]
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text.strip().lower()
    print(f"API response: '{reply}'")
    print(f"Input tokens: {response.usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Output tokens: {response.usage.output_tokens}")
except anthropic.AuthenticationError:
    print("ERROR: Invalid API key. Check ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.")
    sys.exit(1)
except anthropic.RateLimitError:
    print("ERROR: Rate limited. Add credits at console.anthropic.com.")
    sys.exit(1)
except anthropic.APIConnectionError as e:
    print(f"ERROR: Connection failed: {e}. Check proxy/firewall settings.")
    sys.exit(1)

# Step 5: Test streaming
print("Testing streaming...")
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5",
    max_tokens=32,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Count to three: 1,"}]
) as stream:
    tokens = []
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        tokens.append(text)
    print(f"Streamed tokens: {len(tokens)}")

print("\nAnthropic SDK setup verified successfully.")
